content:
From https://lwn.net/SubscriberLink/952029/99c9ecdad7adf323/

Chinner said that he would like to see a reimplementation of the ext2 filesystem in Rust. It is a complete filesystem that makes wide use of the kernel's APIs, but it is still small enough to read and understand. If the Rust APIs can support an ext2 implementation, they will be enough to implement others as well. Meanwhile, the ext2 implementation would be good reference for maintainers, who could compare it to the C version.

So... who's going to work on that :)
I think @Andreas Hindborg is up to it? ;-)
https://github.com/torvalds/linux/tree/9b6de136b5f0158c60844f85286a593cb70fb364/fs/ext2 is only 6k lines of code, most of it in super.c and inode.c. Not small but maybe doable
Especially since a rust reference exists :smile: https://gitlab.com/mordaklavache/ext2
Probably best not to rely on that one because of licensing
interesting, so dual Apache + Mit is not compatible with GPL?
MIT is compatible with GPL, so that is fine.
While it is technically fine, my understanding is that it does not sit well with the wider kernel community?

Reusing code is cool, but as a reference driver, we might need a different implementation: for example, for the on-disk layout, we probably want to use bindgen generated structs instead of pure Rust struct to show how to sync between Rust and C side.

also seems that implementation lacks multiple thread support (I haven’t seen any locks but there is a RefCell) and page cache support (I haven’t seen mmap syscall implementation)? Then probably better start from scratch ourselves.
I could create a branch with Wedson's fs abstractions (not the read-only ones) and go from there.
I wouldn’t actually suggest reusing that crate I linked of course, just a reference for patterns or data structures
Yeah, that repo provides useful information like size of the work, and the pattern you mentioned. Thanks!
@Ariel Miculas are you thinking about going for this?
I'm considering it, but I don't know how much time I'll be able to allocate, and holiday season is around the corner
And I'm wondering whether a read-only ext2 fs would be a good starting point
